title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: The PR adds coupon discount support but ships with critical flaws: a PCI-DSS violation (logging unredacted request bodies), a silent error handler that hides bugs, a correctness bug in the discount calculation, and an over-engineered strategy pattern masquerading as future-proofing. The code also suffers from widespread naming rot (doStuff, theData, tmp, x, result2), type unsafety (as any), and a response that mixes snake_case with camelCase. None of these are close calls — they are foundational problems that will compound.

findings.title: Logging entire request body with card data and PII
findings.message: Line 17 logs JSON.stringify(req.body) to console, which exposes PAN, CVC, email, and customer data in plaintext. This violates PCI-DSS 3.4 (must not log PAN in any form) and 4.1 (cleartext data must be protected). The project ships with a pino logger that has a redaction allowlist for exactly this reason. Remove the console.log entirely; if you need debugging, use the injected logger with selective field logging (e.g., logger.debug({ transactionId }, 'charge initiated')).

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows coupon application failures
findings.message: The try-catch at lines 22-27 catches errors from doStuff but has only a TODO comment, silently dropping the exception. This hides bugs: an invalid coupon code, an out-of-bounds discount, or a future refactoring error will all look like success and charge the full amount with discount_amount=0. Either handle the error meaningfully (log it, rethrow it, return a 400), or remove the try-catch and let it propagate. Never silence an exception that affects business logic.

findings.title: Discount calculation bug: always returns zero
findings.message: Line 24 mutates payload.amount to finalAmount (the discounted amount), then line 36 calculates discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount, which is always 0. Example: if amount=100 and coupon applies a $10 discount, finalAmount=90, payload.amount is set to 90, then 90-90=0. The discount is never shown to the client. Calculate the discount before mutating: const originalAmount = payload.amount; const finalAmount = doStuff(originalAmount, couponCode); then response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;

findings.title: Type assertion 'as any' disables type safety on response
findings.message: Line 35 uses 'const response: any = transaction' to avoid type errors when adding discount_amount and coupon_code. This surrenders type checking on the entire response object — future mutations won't be caught. Instead, extend or intersect the type: 'const response: Transaction & { discountAmount: number; couponCode?: string } = { ...transaction, discountAmount, couponCode }'. Let the compiler help you.

findings.title: Response mixes snake_case and camelCase naming conventions
findings.message: Lines 36-37 add discount_amount and coupon_code (snake_case) to a response object that uses camelCase everywhere else (id, amount, currency, etc.). This breaks the contract: clients expect camelCase. Use discountAmount and couponCode to match the rest of the API.

findings.title: Meaningless function name 'doStuff' on exported API
findings.message: Line 8 imports doStuff, which is an exported entry point for applying coupons. The name tells a caller nothing — they don't know if it validates, applies, or just returns a number. Rename to applyCoupon(amount, couponCode): number. The name is the first thing a reader sees; 'doStuff' is a surrender.

findings.title: Meaningless variable name 'theData'
findings.message: Line 15 names an untrusted request body 'theData', which says nothing. Either extract the specific field you need (const couponCode = req.body.couponCode) or use a meaningful name (const rawPayload = req.body). 'theData' is a placeholder; finish the thought.

findings.title: Over-engineered strategy pattern for a static lookup table
findings.message: Lines 6-50 build an abstract class, two strategy subclasses, and a factory to dispatch 6 hardcoded coupon codes. This is a lookup table wearing a costume. A lookup table doesn't need polymorphism, inheritance, or a factory. Replace with: const COUPONS: Record<string, { kind: 'percent'|'fixed'; value: number }> = { WELCOME10: {kind:'percent',value:0.1}, SUMMER15: {kind:'percent',value:0.15}, ... }; then one function: function applyCoupon(amount, code) { const c = COUPONS[code]; if (!c) return amount; return c.kind==='percent' ? Math.floor(amount*(1-c.value)) : Math.max(0,amount-c.value); } This is 10 lines vs 45, all clarity and zero abstraction rent. When the rules actually become complex (date-bound, tiered), *then* reach for a strategy — and you'll know exactly why.

findings.title: Dead commented-out code should be deleted
findings.message: Lines 48-50 comment out a FREESHIP coupon that 'shipping not used yet'. Commented-out branches rot: no one knows if it's a plan, rollback, or fossil, and every reader has to decide whether to resurrect it. Git remembers the history — delete dead code. If FREESHIP is genuinely needed, add it as a proper case in the lookup when shipping is implemented.

findings.title: Multiple style violations: var, ==, meaningless names, console.log
findings.message: The doStuff function (lines 52-63) mixes bad habits: line 55 uses 'var tmp' (should be 'const'), line 55 names it 'tmp' (meaningless), line 56 uses '==' instead of '===', line 57 uses console.log instead of the injected logger, and line 59 names the result 'result2' (meaningless). Example: 'const strategy = CouponHandlerFactory.build(couponCode); if (strategy === null) { logger.warn({couponCode}, 'unknown coupon'); return amount; } return strategy.apply(amount);' This mirrors the house style: const, ===, meaningful names, logger instead of console.log.

findings.title: Coupon discount feature has no unit tests
findings.message: The test plan marks 'Add unit tests for the coupon math' as a TODO. The discount application (the core business logic of this PR) is completely untested — no tests for the discount calculation, no tests for coupon validation, no tests that the response includes discount_amount with the right value. This is not a follow-up; it's a blocker. Write tests for the discount before merge: happy path (WELCOME10 applies 10%), edge cases (invalid coupon, zero amount, discount > amount), and the response shape (discountAmount is a number, couponCode is optional). A green test run without tests for the feature is false confidence.
